g.text: 🟥 In lock: write() to page cache (~2-6 µs)
🟩 Out of lock: fsync() to disk (~1-10 ms)
uint64_t wal_append(WalWriter* w, uint8_t* rec, size_t len) {
write() syscall | ~1-5 µs (copies to page cache)
T1: A seeks to 1000, starts writing record A (50 bytes)
T2: B seeks to 1000, starts writing record B (30 bytes)
T3: Both write to same offset!
Result: Garbage interleaved bytes at offset 1000
T1: A locks mutex, writes 50 bytes at 1000-1049
T3: A unlocks mutex, returns LSN=1000
T4: B locks mutex, writes 30 bytes at 1050-1079
T5: B unlocks mutex, returns LSN=1050

div.p: Lock held for microseconds, not milliseconds The expensive fsync() (1-10ms) happens outside this lock via group commit. This is why high-throughput is possible: many threads can append concurrently, blocking only for the brief moment of the write() syscall.
LSNs are assigned in lock order, guaranteeing:

ul.li: LSN₁ < LSN₂ implies record₁ was written before record₂
Recovery scans in LSN order = chronological order

div.p: Mutex ensures one thread writes complete record:

ul.li: Header, payload, and CRC are atomic unit
No partial record visible to readers
Thread writes new record with that prev_lsn
Chain is never broken by interleaving
